children.value: Consider the following simple workhorse three-period consumption smoothing model where consumers’ preferences are summarized by constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) utility. In any period the consumer’s instantaneous utility is given by

children.html: u(c)=c1−ρ/(1−ρ)u(c)=c^{1-ρ}/(1-ρ)u(c)=c1−ρ/(1−ρ)

children.value: .  Over three period the agent maximizes utility

children.html: U(c0,c1,c2)=u(c0)+β[δu(c1)+δ2u(c2)]U(c_0, c_1, c_2) =u(c_0) + \beta [\delta u(c_1) + \delta^2 u(c_2)]U(c0​,c1​,c2​)=u(c0​)+β[δu(c1​)+δ2u(c2​)]

children.value: This is a version of the classic

children.html: β−δ\beta-\deltaβ−δ

children.value: quasi-hyperbolic discounting model.  We assume the consumer has an autarky income stream

children.html: y={y0,y1,y2}{y}=\{y_{0},y_{1},y_{2}\}y={y0​,y1​,y2​}

children.value: which defines autarky or reservation utility

children.html: u‾(y)=U(y0,y1,y2) \overline{u}(y) = U(y₀,y₁,y₂)u(y)=U(y0​,y1​,y2​)

children.value: but in general will prefer a smoother consumption profile from contracting on financial markets.
Contracts with and without commitment services

children.label: Contracts with and without commitment services

children.value: Let’s assume at first that financial intermediaries compete to offer a multiperiod contract and can -- at zero cost -- credibly commit to not renegotiate the terms of that contract. For the moment we also assume this contract to be enforceably exclusive in the sense that no other bank is allowed to offer a more attractive additional or alternative contract to the period 1 self.  We’ll relax both assumptions shortly.
The offered contract will maximize the period-0 self’s present value of utility

children.html: U(c0,c1,c2)=u(c0)+β[δu(c1)+δ2u(c2)]U(c_{0},c_{1},c_{2})=u(c_{0})+\beta \left[ \delta u(c_{1})+\delta ^{2}u(c_{2})\right]U(c0​,c1​,c2​)=u(c0​)+β[δu(c1​)+δ2u(c2​)]

children.value: subject to the bank’s zero profit condition or, same thing, consumer budget constraint:

children.html: ∑t=02(yt−ct)(1+r)t=0\sum\limits_{t=0}^{2}\frac{\left( y_{t}-c_{t}\right) }{\left( 1+r\right) ^{t}} = 0t=0∑2​(1+r)t(yt​−ct​)​=0
CfcC^fcCfc

children.value: the consumer may save or borrow, depending on their initial income stream and preferred/feasible smoothed consumption stream available from contracting.
The first order conditions for an optimum are:

children.html: u′(c0)=βδ(1+r)u′(c1)u'(c_0) = \beta \delta (1+r) u'(c_1)u′(c0​)=βδ(1+r)u′(c1​)
u′(c1)=δ(1+r)u′(c2)u'(c_1) = \delta (1+r) u'(c_2)u′(c1​)=δ(1+r)u′(c2​)

children.value: The optimal contract will be the three period consumption profile that brings the consumer to the highest feasible iso-utility surface (analogous to an indifference curve except in 3 dimensions), and that will be at a point where the iso-utility surface is tangent to the zero-profit hyperplane that cuts through endowment point

children.value: into the consumer’s binding budget constraint (the bank’s zero profit condition) the problem can be reduced from three equation (two FOC and the zero profit condition) to two:

children.html: c1=β1ρc0c_1 = \beta^\frac{1}{\rho} c_0c1​=βρ1​c0​
c1=∑y−c02c_1 = \frac{\sum y - c_0}{2}c1​=2∑y−c0​​

children.value: The first equation highlight’s the period-zero self’s present bias --they want to consume more in period zer than in period one--  while the second summarizes that hey want to smooth whatever resources are left to future consumption equally between periods 1 and 2.
